flashcards.a: A tissue is a group of cells that are similar in structure and/or work together to achieve a particular function.

flashcards.q: What is division of labour in multicellular organisms?
flashcards.a: Division of labour is the principle where different cells and tissues are specialised to perform specific functions, increasing efficiency compared to cells that perform all functions.

flashcards.q: Name the two main types of plant tissues.
flashcards.a: Meristematic tissue (dividing, growing tissue) and Permanent tissue (non-dividing, specialised tissue).
Meristematic tissue is composed of actively dividing cells located in specific growth regions of plants, responsible for producing new cells for plant growth.

flashcards.q: Name the three types of meristematic tissue and their locations.
flashcards.a: (1) Apical meristem—at tips of stems and roots, increases length. (2) Lateral meristem (cambium)—in sides of stems and roots, increases girth. (3) Intercalary meristem—near nodes in some plants, elongates internodes.
Differentiation is the process by which meristematic cells take up a specific role and lose the ability to divide, forming permanent tissues with specialised functions.

flashcards.q: Name the two types of permanent plant tissues.
flashcards.a: Simple permanent tissue (cells of same type) and Complex permanent tissue (cells of different types working together).

Parenchyma is simple permanent tissue with living cells, thin cell walls, and large intercellular spaces. Main function: food storage. Can contain chlorophyll (chlorenchyma) or air cavities (aerenchyma).
Chlorenchyma is parenchyma tissue that contains chlorophyll and performs photosynthesis.
Aerenchyma is parenchyma tissue with large air cavities, found in aquatic plants to provide buoyancy and help them float.
Collenchyma is simple permanent tissue with living cells, elongated shape, and irregularly thickened cell walls at corners. Function: provides flexibility and mechanical support for plant parts.
Collenchyma is found in leaf stalks below the epidermis and in stems of climbing plants (tendrils), where flexibility is needed.
Sclerenchyma is simple permanent tissue composed of dead cells with thick, lignified cell walls. Function: provides strength and rigidity to plant parts.

flashcards.q: What characteristic of sclerenchyma cells makes them strong?
flashcards.a: Sclerenchyma cells have thickened walls due to lignin deposition. Walls are so thick that often no internal space remains inside the cell, providing maximum rigidity.

flashcards.q: Compare the flexibility of collenchyma and sclerenchyma.
flashcards.a: Collenchyma is flexible (living cells, adaptable walls) allowing bending without breaking. Sclerenchyma is rigid (dead cells, thick lignified walls) and does not allow bending.

flashcards.q: Give an example of a plant part made of sclerenchyma.
flashcards.a: The hard husk of coconut is made of sclerenchymatous tissue. Also found in hard coverings of seeds and nuts.
Epidermis is the outermost protective layer of plant parts, usually a single layer of living cells with thick walls and a waxy cuticle for protection.

flashcards.q: What is the function of the cuticle?
flashcards.a: The cuticle is a waxy, water-resistant layer secreted on the outer surface of epidermis. Functions: reduces water loss, prevents mechanical injury, and prevents invasion by parasitic fungi.

flashcards.q: What are stomata and where are they found?
flashcards.a: Stomata are small pores in the epidermis (mainly on leaves) through which gas exchange occurs. Each stoma is surrounded by two kidney-shaped guard cells.

flashcards.q: What is the function of guard cells?
flashcards.a: Guard cells surround each stoma and control its opening and closing by changing their turgor pressure in response to environmental conditions like light, humidity, and CO₂ levels.
Root hairs are long, hair-like projections of epidermal cells on roots that greatly increase the surface area for water and mineral absorption.
Cork is a secondary protective tissue formed on mature plants. It consists of dead cells with thick, waterproof walls containing suberin. Replaces epidermis in older stems and roots.
Suberin is a waxy substance found in cork cell walls that makes them impervious (waterproof) to water and gases, providing protection and insulation.
Epidermis: single layer, living cells, on young plants, has stomata and root hairs. Cork: multiple layers, dead cells, on mature plants, no stomata, waterproof, acts as insulator.
Xylem is complex permanent tissue composed of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibres. Function: transports water and mineral salts vertically from roots to leaves.

flashcards.q: What are the components of xylem tissue?
flashcards.a: (1) Tracheids and vessels—conduct water and minerals. (2) Xylem parenchyma—stores food. (3) Xylem fibres—provide mechanical support.

flashcards.q: Why are most xylem cells dead at maturity?
flashcards.a: Xylem cells are mostly dead because water transport occurs through physical forces (transpiration pull, root pressure), not active transport. Dead cells provide structural support and have thick walls.
Phloem is complex permanent tissue composed of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibres, and phloem parenchyma. Function: transports food and organic substances throughout the plant.

flashcards.q: What are the components of phloem tissue?
flashcards.a: (1) Sieve tubes—conduct food. (2) Companion cells—regulate transport. (3) Phloem fibres—provide support. (4) Phloem parenchyma—store food.

flashcards.q: Why must phloem cells be living?
flashcards.a: Phloem cells must be living because food transport requires active transport, which needs ATP and metabolic machinery. Living cells can actively pump sugars against concentration gradients.
A vascular bundle is a group of xylem and phloem tissues organised together in plants, found in stems, roots, and leaf veins.

flashcards.q: Name the four main types of animal tissues.
flashcards.a: (1) Epithelial tissue—protection and lining. (2) Connective tissue—support and connection. (3) Muscular tissue—movement. (4) Nervous tissue—control and coordination.

flashcards.q: What are the general characteristics of epithelial tissue?
flashcards.a: Epithelial cells are tightly packed in continuous sheets with minimal intercellular space. They form a barrier at interfaces with external and internal environments.

flashcards.q: Name six types of epithelial tissue.
flashcards.a: (1) Simple squamous—thin, flat cells for transport. (2) Stratified squamous—layers for protection. (3) Cuboidal—cube-shaped for support. (4) Columnar—tall cells for absorption. (5) Ciliated columnar—with cilia for mucus movement. (6) Glandular—secretes substances.

flashcards.q: Where is simple squamous epithelium found and what is its function?
flashcards.a: Simple squamous epithelium is found in blood vessel linings and lung alveoli. Function: allows rapid substance transport across a thin, permeable surface.

flashcards.q: Where is stratified squamous epithelium found and what is its function?
flashcards.a: Stratified squamous epithelium is found in skin, oesophagus, and mouth lining. Function: protection against wear, tear, and pathogen invasion through multiple layers.

flashcards.q: Where is cuboidal epithelium found and what is its function?
flashcards.a: Cuboidal epithelium is found in kidney tubules and salivary gland ducts. Function: provides mechanical support and participates in secretion/reabsorption.

flashcards.q: Where is columnar epithelium found and what is its function?
flashcards.a: Columnar epithelium is found lining the intestines. Function: increases surface area for absorption of nutrients through its tall shape.

flashcards.q: What is ciliated columnar epithelium and where is it found?
flashcards.a: Ciliated columnar epithelium has hair-like cilia on the surface that move to push mucus and debris. Found in respiratory tract.
Glandular epithelium consists of epithelial cells specialised for secretion of substances like enzymes, hormones, and mucus.
Connective tissues are composed of loosely spaced cells embedded in an intercellular matrix. They connect, support, and bind other tissues throughout the body.

flashcards.q: What is blood and what are its components?
flashcards.a: Blood is a fluid connective tissue. Components: (1) Red blood cells (RBCs)—carry oxygen. (2) White blood cells (WBCs)—fight infections. (3) Platelets—assist in clotting. (4) Plasma—liquid matrix carrying nutrients, hormones, and waste.
Plasma is the liquid matrix of blood containing water, proteins, salts, and hormones. It carries dissolved substances throughout the body.
Bone is a hard, rigid connective tissue with cells embedded in a matrix of calcium and phosphorus compounds. Function: provides structural support and protection of organs.

flashcards.q: What are the functions of bone tissue?
flashcards.a: Functions: (1) Forms framework supporting body. (2) Anchors muscles for movement. (3) Protects internal organs. (4) Stores minerals (calcium, phosphorus).
Cartilage is a flexible connective tissue with cells widely spaced in a solid protein-sugar matrix. More flexible than bone but firm and supportive.

flashcards.q: Where is cartilage found in the body?
flashcards.a: Cartilage is found at joints (reduces friction), in the nose, ear, trachea, and larynx—locations where flexibility is needed.

flashcards.q: How does cartilage differ from bone in flexibility?
flashcards.a: Cartilage can be folded and bent without breaking due to its flexible protein-sugar matrix. Bone cannot bend—it is rigid due to calcium-phosphorus matrix.
A ligament is an elastic connective tissue with great strength that connects bone to bone. Function: allows some movement at joints while providing stability.
A tendon is a fibrous connective tissue with great strength and limited flexibility that connects muscle to bone. Function: transfers force from muscle to bone enabling movement.
Ligaments: connect bone-to-bone, elastic and strong, allow joint movement. Tendons: connect muscle-to-bone, strong but less flexible, transfer muscle force to bone.
Areolar tissue is a loose connective tissue found between skin and muscles, around blood vessels and nerves, and in bone marrow. Functions: fills spaces, supports organs, aids tissue repair.
Adipose tissue is connective tissue specialised for fat storage found below skin and between organs. Functions: stores energy as fat, provides insulation, cushions organs.
Muscular tissue consists of elongated cells called muscle fibres containing contractile proteins. Function: contraction and relaxation causes movement of body parts.

flashcards.q: What are the three types of muscle tissue?
flashcards.a: (1) Striated muscle—voluntary skeletal movement. (2) Smooth muscle—involuntary internal functions. (3) Cardiac muscle—heart pumping.
Striated muscle: long, cylindrical, unbranched, multinucleate cells. Shows alternate light and dark bands (striations). Voluntary—contracts by conscious will. Attached to bones for body movement.
Striated muscle is mostly attached to bones and is found in limbs (arms, legs), face, and trunk—enabling voluntary body movement.
Smooth muscle: spindle-shaped, uninucleate cells. No visible striations (unstriated). Involuntary—works automatically without conscious control. Undergoes slow, sustained contraction.
Smooth muscle is found controlling involuntary movements: alimentary canal (food movement), blood vessels (contraction/relaxation), iris (pupil control), ureters, and lung bronchi.
Cardiac muscle: cylindrical, branched, uninucleate cells. Shows faint striations. Involuntary—rhythmic contraction throughout life. Never fatigues or tires.

flashcards.q: What is the specific function of cardiac muscle?
flashcards.a: Cardiac muscle pumps blood throughout the body by rhythmic contraction and relaxation. Works continuously without fatigue throughout life.

flashcards.q: Compare the three muscle types in terms of structure and function.
flashcards.a: Striated: long multinucleate cylindrical, voluntary, rapid precise movement. Smooth: spindle-shaped uninucleate, involuntary, slow sustained movement. Cardiac: branched uninucleate, involuntary, continuous rhythmic pumping.
Nervous tissue is composed of neurons (nerve cells) specialised for receiving stimuli and transmitting signals rapidly throughout the body.

A neuron is the basic functional unit of nervous tissue consisting of: (1) Cell body—nucleus and cytoplasm. (2) Axon—single long extension conducting impulses away. (3) Dendrites—multiple short extensions receiving impulses.

flashcards.q: What is the function of dendrites?
flashcards.a: Dendrites are short, branched extensions of neurons that receive signals (nerve impulses) from other neurons or sensory receptors and conduct them toward the cell body.

flashcards.q: What is the function of an axon?
flashcards.a: An axon is a long, single extension of a neuron that conducts nerve impulses away from the cell body toward other neurons or muscle cells. Can be up to 1 metre long.
A nerve impulse is the electrical or chemical signal that travels along the axon of a neuron, allowing rapid communication between different parts of the nervous system.
A synapse is the junction between two neurons where the nerve impulse from one neuron is transmitted to the dendrite of the next neuron through neurotransmitters.
A nerve is a bundle of many nerve fibres (axons) held together by connective tissue. Transmits nerve impulses between the brain/spinal cord and body parts.

flashcards.q: How does the nerve-muscle system enable rapid response to stimuli?
flashcards.a: Nervous tissue rapidly detects stimuli and transmits signals (up to 100 m/s). Muscular tissue contracts in milliseconds. Together they enable animals to respond to threats and opportunities in milliseconds.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between plant growth and animal growth?
flashcards.a: Plant growth: localised to specific regions (meristems); growth from tips and sides only. Animal growth: uniform throughout body; cells can divide everywhere, though rate slows with age.

flashcards.q: Why do plants have dead support tissue (sclerenchyma) while animals have living muscles?
flashcards.a: Plants are stationary and need rigid, dead support tissue for structural strength. Animals move and need living, flexible muscle tissue for rapid movement and response.

flashcards.q: What is the significance of the root tip experiment?
flashcards.a: The root tip experiment demonstrates that plant growth occurs only in specific localised regions (apical meristem). Cutting the root tip stops all growth because meristematic cells are removed.

flashcards.q: How do tissues show adaptation to function?
flashcards.a: Tissue structure reflects function: epithelial cells tightly packed for protection, connective cells loosely spaced for support, muscle cells elongated for contraction, nerve cells have long axons for signal transmission.

flashcards.q: What principle defines tissue organisation in multicellular organisms?
flashcards.a: Division of labour—cells and tissues specialise in specific functions, increasing efficiency. Each tissue performs its job very efficiently, contributing to overall organism function.
